title: iptables rules not cleared when publishing ports in a network
body:
Hi,

i'm running podman on centos/8

podman version
Version:      3.2.3
API Version:  3.2.3
Go Version:   go1.15.14
Built:        Tue Aug 10 20:55:16 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64

CentOS Linux release 8.4.2105
Derived from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8.4

Here, i am using (simplified example) an nginx container and a pgadmin container
The nginx container is started first, the pgadmin container later on.

I am using a container-network (podman network create name),  the containers are connected to this network (--net)
Nginx has a port publishing of nginx ports 8080 and 8443, pgadmin does not publish any ports (pgadmin access is proxied by nginx)

With a nginx resolver and variables, nginx queries e.g. 10.89.0.1 for the pgadmin-container name, even if pgadmin is started later and can proxy requests to it.

In the background, iptables rules are (presumably) handled by a podman plugin
```
podman network ls
NETWORK ID    NAME                       VERSION     PLUGINS
2f259bab93aa  podman                     0.4.0       bridge,portmap,firewall,tuning
3fb6548d951e  hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust  0.4.0       bridge,portmap,firewall,tuning,dnsname
```

iptables -S -t nat shows
```
-A PREROUTING -m addrtype --dst-type LOCAL -j CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT
-A POSTROUTING -m comment --comment "CNI portfwd requiring masquerade" -j CNI-HOSTPORT-MASQ
-A POSTROUTING -s 10.89.0.3/32 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540
-A OUTPUT -m addrtype --dst-type LOCAL -j CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT
-A CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540 -d 10.89.0.0/24 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j ACCEPT
-A CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540 ! -d 224.0.0.0/4 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -m comment --comment "CNI portfwd masquerade mark" -j MARK --set-xmark 0x2000/0x2000
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-MASQ -m mark --mark 0x2000/0x2000 -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -m multiport --dports 8080,8443 -j CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.3:8080
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.3:8443
```

so far, so fine.

But, when i am stopping the containers and remove the network,  the iptables rules stay put and are not cleared.

Which is a problem when re-creating the network, containers and port publishing. Now, the forwarded ports are not accessible any more, a `curl localhost:8080` is not replied to any more 
```
* Rebuilt URL to: localhost:8080/
*   Trying ::1...
* TCP_NODELAY set
* connect to ::1 port 8080 failed: Connection refused
*   Trying 127.0.0.1...
* TCP_NODELAY set
* connect to 127.0.0.1 port 8080 failed: No route to host
* Failed to connect to localhost port 8080: No route to host
* Closing connection 0
curl: (7) Failed to connect to localhost port 8080: No route to host
```

iptables then looks like this
```
-A PREROUTING -m addrtype --dst-type LOCAL -j CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT
-A POSTROUTING -m comment --comment "CNI portfwd requiring masquerade" -j CNI-HOSTPORT-MASQ
-A POSTROUTING -s 10.89.0.3/32 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540
-A POSTROUTING -s 10.89.0.4/32 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"96b0bf8434caa23d6c4910f1712a5bfbdc0e35f31a800032d97619152ae07f48\"" -j CNI-64190318340cd980cdd4afab
-A POSTROUTING -s 10.89.0.6/32 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"d38bd4c2e5ff36a75470b376972ca080076ccb2d73886a642b8833cf81d5fc02\"" -j CNI-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0726
-A OUTPUT -m addrtype --dst-type LOCAL -j CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT
-A CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540 -d 10.89.0.0/24 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j ACCEPT
-A CNI-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f540 ! -d 224.0.0.0/4 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK -m comment --comment "CNI portfwd masquerade mark" -j MARK --set-xmark 0x2000/0x2000
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-MASQ -m mark --mark 0x2000/0x2000 -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"140b404c495a1b67bbb6ec2a252eee5d5506ddfe4e880244964332c68b0d9eb9\"" -m multiport --dports 8080,8443 -j CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f
-A CNI-HOSTPORT-DNAT -p tcp -m comment --comment "dnat name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"d38bd4c2e5ff36a75470b376972ca080076ccb2d73886a642b8833cf81d5fc02\"" -m multiport --dports 8080,8443 -j CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.3:8080
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-421920a47d0ae6ebfc32f -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.3:8443
-A CNI-64190318340cd980cdd4afab -d 10.89.0.0/24 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"96b0bf8434caa23d6c4910f1712a5bfbdc0e35f31a800032d97619152ae07f48\"" -j ACCEPT
-A CNI-64190318340cd980cdd4afab ! -d 224.0.0.0/4 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"96b0bf8434caa23d6c4910f1712a5bfbdc0e35f31a800032d97619152ae07f48\"" -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0726 -d 10.89.0.0/24 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"d38bd4c2e5ff36a75470b376972ca080076ccb2d73886a642b8833cf81d5fc02\"" -j ACCEPT
-A CNI-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0726 ! -d 224.0.0.0/4 -m comment --comment "name: \"hisinone_net_cu-ralf-cust\" id: \"d38bd4c2e5ff36a75470b376972ca080076ccb2d73886a642b8833cf81d5fc02\"" -j MASQUERADE
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8080 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.6:8080
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -s 10.89.0.0/24 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -s 127.0.0.1/32 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j CNI-HOSTPORT-SETMARK
-A CNI-DN-8252ef108fdbd739b61f0 -p tcp -m tcp --dport 8443 -j DNAT --to-destination 10.89.0.6:8443
```


My current workaround is to do a flush of all iptables rules
```
iptables -t nat -F
```
followed by a 
```
podman network reload --all
```

Now, the ports 8080/8443 are again accessible.

Question:
Wouldn't it be better, if - after a `podman network rm XXX` the iptables entries would be cleared as well?
Or am i missing something here?

Looking forward for advice

comments.body: This should be fixed in podman 3.3. Running `unset XDG_RUNTIME_DIR` before you run any podman commands should also work as workaround.

@Luap99 i tried with the unset command, but the iptables cleanup does not happen - nor does it help when restarting containers.
At the moment, only flush and network reload seem to help
You have to use the unset command before you start any containers.
Yes, that did the trick! Thanks
